claims.claim: As cycle count increased from 0 to 60, ignition time of LIBs diminished from 301 s to 191 s, and the interval between deflation and ignition contracted from 21 s to 1 s.
claims.quote: As the cycle count rose from 0 to 60, the ignition time diminished from 301 s to 191 s, the interval between deflation and ignition contracted from 21 s to 1 s

claims.claim: After 120 aging cycles, NCA battery capacity decreased by 21.1% and internal resistance increased by 2.65 mΩ compared to fresh batteries.
claims.quote: After 120 cycles, the capacity of the battery decreased by 21.1％, and the internal resistance also increased by 2.65 mΩ.

claims.claim: When battery SOH falls to 80% or below, internal resistance increases greatly, affecting safety performance of the battery.
claims.quote: when the battery SOH≦80 %, the battery's internal resistance will increase greatly, affecting the safety performance of the battery

claims.claim: For NCA batteries aged 120 cycles, TR onset time for 100% SOC batteries was 324 s faster than for 30% SOC batteries, and TR onset temperature decreased from 225.5°C at 30% SOC to 170.7°C at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: the TR onset time of 100 % SOC batteries is 324 s faster than that of 30 % SOC batteries, while at the same time, the TR onset temperature is also significantly reduced, from 225.5℃ for 30 % SOC to 170.7℃ for 100 % SOC

claims.claim: For NCA batteries aged 120 cycles, CO concentration in TR gas increased with increasing SOC, while CO2 concentration decreased with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: the concentration of CO in the TR gas increased with the increase of SOC, while for CO2 was the opposite was observed, its concentration decreased with the increase of SOC

claims.claim: The maximum surface temperature of NCM523 batteries during rapid penetration-induced thermal runaway under 100°C critical thermal load increases with SOC, ranging from 122.4°C at 0% SOC to 692.8°C at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: The specific data are as follows: 0 % SOC (122.4℃), 10 % SOC (123.4℃), 20 % SOC (124.9℃), 25 % SOC (146.3℃), 30 % SOC (380.5℃), 50 % SOC (439.5℃), 75 % SOC (569.7℃), 100 % SOC (692.8℃). The experimental data show that Tmax is positively correlated with SOC

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate of NCM523 batteries during rapid penetration-induced thermal runaway under 100°C critical thermal load increases with SOC: 0%, 10%, and 20% SOC batteries exhibit 2.2°C/s, 2.39°C/s, and 2.51°C/s respectively; 25% SOC reaches 7.31°C/s; and 30%, 50%, 75%, and 100% SOC batteries reach 33.8°C/s, 78.2°C/s, 85.1°C/s, and 93.5°C/s respectively.
claims.quote: the temperature rise rates of 0 %, 10 % and 20 % SOC batteries are 2.2℃/s, 2.39℃/s and 2.51℃/s respectively, the temperature rise rate of 25 % SOC batteries is 7.31℃/s, the temperature rise rates of 30 %, 50 %, 75 % and 100 % SOC batteries are 33.8℃/s, 78.2℃/s, 85.1℃/s and 93.5℃/s respectively

claims.claim: Under rapid penetration at 100°C critical thermal load, NCM523 batteries at 0–20% SOC are in mild TR state (temperature rise rate 1–5°C/s), 25% SOC is in moderate TR state (temperature rise rate 5–10°C/s), and 30–100% SOC are in severe TR state (temperature rise rate exceeding 10°C/s).
claims.quote: it can be determined that 0 % − 20 % SOC batteries are in a mild TR state, 25 % SOC battery is in a moderate TR, and 30 % − 100 % SOC batteries are all in a severe TR state. This result clearly shows that the SOC level is positively correlated with the severity of TR.

claims.claim: Rapid penetration of NCM523 batteries preheated to 100°C significantly reduced the thermal runaway trigger temperature compared to overheating alone; under overheating, TR trigger temperatures were 169.1°C, 178.2°C, 185.9°C, and 194.6°C for 100%, 75%, 50%, and 30% SOC respectively.
claims.quote: The experimental results showed that the TR trigger temperatures of batteries with 100 %, 75 %, 50 % and 30 % SOC states under overheating condition were 169.1℃, 178.2℃, 185.9℃ and 194.6℃, respectively. These results indicate that rapid penetration significantly reduced the TR trigger temperature threshold of NCM523 battery.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, the eruption mode of severe TR NCM523 batteries under rapid penetration at 100°C critical thermal load transitions from smoke diffusion (30% SOC) to transverse spark jet (50% SOC), longitudinal spark jet (75% SOC), and flame jet (100% SOC), with the spark jet area of the 100% SOC battery being 41.44% higher than that of the 50% SOC battery.
claims.quote: the eruption behavior of severe TR batteries experienced smoke diffusion mode, transverse spark jet mode, longitudinal spark jet mode and flame jet mode, and the spark jet area also increased significantly, with the maximum area increased by 41.44 %

claims.claim: Installing an explosion vent in an 80 L test chamber reduced TR gas explosion overpressure by more than 93%, with average maximum overpressure decreasing from 383 kPa to a range of 7.2 kPa to 27 kPa.
claims.quote: after the explosion vent was installed, Pmax was significantly reduced, with the average maximum overpressure decreasing from 383 kPa to a range of 7.2 kPa ~ 27 kPa, representing a reduction of over 93 %

claims.claim: As the dimensionless venting coefficient Kv decreases from 74.26 to 6.19, the average maximum overpressure peak drops from 27 kPa to 7.2 kPa and the average deflagration index decreases from 220.73 kPa·m/s to 3.96 kPa·m/s.
claims.quote: as Kv decreases from 74.26 to 6.19, the average maximum overpressure peak drops from 27 kPa to 7.2 kPa, and the average deflagration index decreases from 220.73 kPa•m/s to 3.96 kPa•m/s

claims.claim: The venting efficiency index Ks first increases and then decreases as vent area increases, with optimal venting performance achieved at Kv = 8.25.
claims.quote: Ks first increases and then decreases as the explosion vent area increases, with the optimal venting design determined to be Kv = 8.25

claims.claim: The TR gas explosion of a 4 Ah lithium iron phosphate battery in an 80 L closed chamber produced an average maximum overpressure of 0.38 MPa, an average maximum overpressure rise rate of 5.37 MPa/s, and an average explosion propagation speed of 634 m/s, indicating detonation.
claims.quote: the average maximum overpressure of the gas released during TR of the 4000mAh LIB in the explosion process was 0.38 MPa, the average maximum overpressure rise rate was 5.37 MPa/s, and the average deflagration index was 2.31 m•MPa/s. The average TNT equivalent of TR explosion was 7.4 g. The average explosion propagation speed was 634 m/s (I.e. supersonic), indicating that the TR explosion of such cells is detonation.

claims.claim: When the vent area is small, higher vent opening pressure causes delayed opening of the explosion vent, leading to prolonged accumulation and increased concentration of combustible gases, and after venting some high-temperature unburned gases may mix with external air to form a combustible cloud that risks secondary explosion.
claims.quote: When the vent area is small, the higher opening pressure causes delayed opening of the explosion vent. This leads to prolonged accumulation and increased concentration of combustible gases within the test chamber. After venting, some high-temperature unburned gases mix with external air to form a new combustible cloud. If the combustible cloud is exposed to high temperatures on the surface of external equipment or electric sparks, it may trigger a secondary explosion.

claims.claim: H2 content in TR gas increases with charging rate: 61% at 2C, 53% at 1.5C, and 50% at 1C for overcharged LFP batteries.
claims.quote: the content of H2 also shows an increasing trend. The H2 contents for 2C, 1.5C and 1C correspond to 61%, 53% and 50%, respectively.

claims.claim: As charging rate increases during overcharge of LFP batteries, the lower explosion limit of the released gas mixture decreases, ranging from 7.26% at 1C to 6.54% at 1.5C to 5.99% at 2C.
claims.quote: As the charging rate decreasees, both the lower and upper explosion limits increase, ranging from 5.99% to 77.28%, 6.54%–77.77%, 7.26%–82.71% (corresponding to 2C, 1.5C, 1C, respectively).

claims.claim: At 0.5C charging rate, the LFP battery does not undergo thermal runaway but only experiences exhaust/venting, while at 1C, 1.5C, and 2C the battery reaches thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the 2C, 1.5C, and 1C batteries reach TR, while the 0.5C battery only experiences exhaust.

claims.claim: Maximum tank pressure during overcharge increases with charging rate: 16 kPa at 2C, 11 kPa at 1.5C, 10 kPa at 1C, and 3 kPa at 0.5C.
claims.quote: the maximum pressure of the battery under overcharge at different rates reaches 16, 11, 10, and 3 kPa, corresponding to 2C, 1.5C, 1C, 0.5C, respectively.

claims.claim: H2 is detected prior to CO and CO2 during TR flue gas emission, indicating H2 is an effective characteristic gas for early thermal runaway warning.
claims.quote: the peak of H2 appears earlier than CO and CO2, suggesting its potential as a superior TR warning signal compared to other gases.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature rise rate during overcharge-induced TR reaches 25.07 °C/s at 2C, compared to 12.705 °C/s at 1.5C, 15.275 °C/s at 1C, and 1.96 °C/s at 0.5C.
claims.quote: The temperature rise rate of 2C is a significant 25.07 ◦C/s (detailed parameters are shown in Table 2), indicating a serious TR risk under high charging rate conditions.

claims.claim: Battery failure accounts for more than 70% of electric vehicle fire accidents.
claims.quote: Among electric vehicle (EV) fire accidents, battery failure accounts for more than 70 %

claims.claim: In nail penetration, the contact resistance is usually several orders of magnitude larger than the nail internal resistance, so the total short-circuit resistance is dominated by contact resistance.
claims.quote: In the nail penetration scenario, the contact resistance is usually several orders of magnitude larger than the nail internal resistance, so the total resistance is dominated by the contact resistance

claims.claim: Under inhomogeneous contact resistance assumption, maximum heat production during nail penetration was always 300% higher than under the uniform assumption.
claims.quote: the maximum heat production under the inhomogeneous assumption was always 300 % higher than that under the uniformity assumption

claims.claim: The initial SOC of a lithium-ion battery is positively correlated with its gas production during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the initial SOC of the battery is positively correlated with its gas production

claims.claim: The lower flammability limit (LFL) of battery vent gas decreases with increasing SOC because at low SOC thermal runaway is less violent and produces more CO2, requiring more fuel relative to O2 for ignition.
claims.quote: In general, LFL decreased with the increase of SOC. Because at low SOC, thermal runaway is less violent and electrolyte can react adequately, which produces a large amount of CO2. In this case, more fuel relative to O2 is needed for the ignition, corresponding to a higher LFL, and vice versa

claims.claim: As H2 volume concentration increases from 10% to 30%, the LFL of BVG decreases from 15.65% to 12.3% and the UFL increases from 50.6% to 56.92% at 300 K and 100 kPa, broadening the flammable range.
claims.quote: As the H2 volume concentration increases from 10 % to 30 %, the LFL decreases from 15.65 % to 12.3 %, while the UFL increases from 50.6 % to 56.92 %.

claims.claim: As CO2 volume concentration increases from 20% to 70%, the LFL of BVG increases from 10.32% to 37.76% and the UFL rises from 51.85% to 64.2%, with the LFL increase being more pronounced than the UFL increase, narrowing the flammable range.
claims.quote: As the volume concentration of CO2 increases from 20 % to 70 %, the LFL of the gas mixture increases from 10.32 % to 37.76 %, and the UFL rises from 51.85 % to 64.2 %. The increase in the LFL is more pronounced than the UFL, indicating that the LFL is more sensitive to the changes in CO2 content. The increase in CO2 content significantly narrows the flammable range.

claims.claim: As CH4 content increases from 2% to 10%, both the LFL and UFL of BVG decrease (LFL from 15.26% to 13.05%, UFL from 53.22% to 43.7%), and the flammable range progressively narrows.
claims.quote: As the content of CH4 increases from 2 % to 10 %, the LFL decreases from 15.26 % to 13.05 %, while the UFL decreases from 53.22 % to 43.7 %... as the content of CH4 increases, the flammable range of BVG progressively narrows

claims.claim: Among NCA, LFP, and LCO battery vent gases, the flammable range of NCA battery is the widest, followed by LFP and LCO batteries, indicating that the explosion risk of gases vented from NCA battery thermal runaway is the greatest.
claims.quote: the flammable range of BVG in NCA batteries is the broadest, trailed by that in LFP and LCO batteries. It also suggests that the explosion risk of the gases vented from NCA battery thermal runaway is the greatest.

claims.claim: As initial temperature increases from 300 K to 500 K, the UFL of BVG increases and the LFL decreases for both NCA and LFP batteries, and the increase of UFL and decrease of LFL versus initial temperature are nearly linear regardless of SOC and battery type.
claims.quote: the UFL of BVG increased with the raising initial temperature rises from 300 K to 500 K for the NCA and LFP batteries, while the LFL decreased in the same condition... The increase trends of UFL and the decrease trend of LFL versus the increase of initial temperature are nearly linear, which is not affected by the SOC and battery type.

claims.claim: At 100.8 kPa, the average ignition time for single batteries decreased with increasing SOC: 364 s at 0% SOC, 342 s at 50% SOC, and 315 s at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: the average time to ignition decreases with the increasing SOC under the same radiation exposure... the average ignition time in Hefei is longer than in Lhasa

claims.claim: At lower ambient pressure (64.3 kPa, Lhasa), batteries ignited faster than at higher ambient pressure (100.8 kPa, Hefei) under the same heating conditions.
claims.quote: The ejection in Lhasa will appear earlier than that in Hefei due to its lower pressure required for the rupture... the increasing of the altitude or reduction in ambient pressure causes the battery to be ignited faster

claims.claim: Peak HRR values for single batteries at 100.8 kPa were 2.86, 5.1, and 2.84 kW for 0%, 50%, and 100% SOC respectively, showing a non-monotonic relationship with SOC.
claims.quote: The peak HRRs values corresponding to 0%, 50% and 100% SOC were 2.86, 5.1 and 2.84 kW respectively at 100.8 kPa

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the CO/CO2 ratio increased sharply compared to lower SOC values, indicating a significant decrease in combustion efficiency at full charge.
claims.quote: The sharp increase in CO/CO2 ratio of 100% SOC battery indicates an obvious decrease in combustion efﬁciency.

claims.claim: The total heat release of bundled batteries increases with cell number in a power function Q(n) = an^b, where the coefficient a is pressure dependent: a = 16.99 at 64.3 kPa and a = 22.57 at 100.8 kPa.
claims.quote: The total heat release in the bundle increases with the battery numbers in a power function. The coefﬁcient of the proportionality is pressure dependent... Value 16.99 [a at 64.3 kPa]... 22.57 [a at 100.8 kPa]

claims.claim: At 0% SOC with 400-W heating power and at 100% SOC with 100-W heating power, only the SVO minor pressure occurred with no thermal runaway or intense major impact pressure.
claims.quote: Tests of a 0% SOC battery with 400-W heating power and a 100% SOC battery with 100-W heating power were also conducted, but both had only the SVO minor pressure and no thermal runaway or intense major impact pressure.

claims.claim: The maximum impact pressure during the intense ejection stage increased with increasing battery SOC, reaching 10.8, 11.9, 19.2, 86.5, and 107.2 kPa at SOCs of 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100%, respectively, under 400-W heating power.
claims.quote: The ejection of the thermal runaway generated maximum overpressures of 10.8, 11.9, and 19.2 kPa when the battery SOC was of 20%, 40%, and 60%, respectively. However, thermal runaway occurred dramatically at a high SOC with the maximum impact pressure soaring to 86.5 and 107.2 kPa when the battery SOCs were 80% and 100%, respectively.

claims.claim: The maximum pressure-rise rate during the intense ejection stage increased dramatically at high SOC: rates were 42.6, 64.6, and 49.2 kPa/s at 20%, 40%, and 60% SOC, but soared to 673.6 and 694.4 kPa/s at 80% and 100% SOC, respectively, under 400-W heating power.
claims.quote: the maximum pressure-rise rates were 42.6, 64.6, and 49.2 kPa∙s−1 ... when the thermal runaway occurred at a 20%, 40%, and 60% SOC, respectively. However, the maximum rates soared to 673.6 and 694.4 kPa∙s−1 ... with an 80% and 100% SOC, respectively.

claims.claim: The initial pressure-rise temperature and maximum pressure temperature during the intense ejection stage declined with increasing battery SOC, reaching as low as 241.6 °C and 241.8 °C respectively at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: the initial pressure-rise temperatures (TIPR) reached 318.3, 285.2, 273.4, 262.3, and 241.6 °C, and the maximum pressure temperatures (TMP) were 328.1, 286.6, 276.0, 263.5, and 241.8 °C with battery SOCs of 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100%, respectively. The initial pressure-rise temperature and the maximum pressure temperature declined with an increase in the battery SOC.

claims.claim: At SOCs of 20%, 40%, and 60% with 400-W heating power, the injury risk from impact pressure was below the LD1 curve, while at all other tested conditions (higher SOC or different heating powers) the injury risk was above LD1 and below LD10, with the result close to LD10 at 100% SOC heated at 350 W or 400 W.
claims.quote: the injury risk of the impact pressures from thermal runaway with LIB SOCs of 20%, 40%, and 60% and heating power of 400 W were below the LD1 curve. However, the injury risk of all other conditions was above the LD1 curve and below the LD10 curve. The test result was close to the LD10 curve when a 100% SOC battery was externally heated at 350 W or 400 W.

claims.claim: The average temperature at which cells start safety venting was 193°C under a heating ramp of 11°C/min.
claims.quote: The average temperature where the cells start the safety venting was found at 193 ◦C.

claims.claim: The average exothermic onset temperature across all nine experiments was 238°C.
claims.quote: The exothermic onset was, on average, 238 ◦C.

claims.claim: The average maximum temperature achieved on the cell surface across the nine experiments was 567°C.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature achieved in the cell surface was average for the nine experiments, 567 ◦C.

claims.claim: LCO initiated flame by a hot spot in the vent cap, while NMC ejected hot solid particles that ignited the flammable gas; LFP did not show any flame propagation for any of the three SOC levels tested.
claims.quote: The high-speed camera recording shows that the LCO initiated the flame by a hot spot in the vent cap while the NMC ejected hot solid particles that burned the flammable gas. The LFP did not show any flame propagation for the three SOC tested.

claims.claim: Increasing SOC decreases the venting temperature and increases the maximum self-heat rate for LCO, NMC, and LFP cells.
claims.quote: The temperature at the start of the thermal runaway decreased, and the rate of temperature rise in the cell increased with SOC.

claims.claim: When charging current increases from 1C to 6C for 50 Ah LiFePO4 batteries, total gas generation increases from 43.0 L to 171.5 L, which is approximately 4 times larger.
claims.quote: the total gas generation increases from 43.0 L to 171.5 L when the charging current increases from 1C to 6C

claims.claim: When battery capacity increases from 50 Ah to 280 Ah under 1C overcharge, normalized gas release increases from 0.7 L/Ah to 1.7 L/Ah.
claims.quote: The normalized gas release increased from 0.7 to 1.7 L/Ah, when battery capacity increases from 50 to 280 Ah

claims.claim: Under 1C overcharge in confined space (50 Ah battery), before stage IV (thermal runaway onset), 41.7% of heat and only 9.3% of gases had been released, indicating heat generation process was faster than gas generation.
claims.quote: Before stage IV, 41.7 % heat and 9.3 % gases have been released, respectively. The heat generation process was faster than the gas generation, and most of the gases were generated after the TR was triggered, namely stage IV.

claims.claim: The maximum pressure rise rate standardized coefficient reached a maximum of 25.6 for the 280 Ah battery at 1C, 1 kN, making it the most sensitive parameter to test conditions among all evaluated TR parameters.
claims.quote: the maximum pressure rise rate is found to be the most sensitive parameter to the test conditions, which reaches a maximum of 25.6

claims.claim: When pre-load force exceeded 2 kN for 100 Ah batteries under 1C overcharge, total gas generation increased from 141.3 L to approximately 190 L and then remained approximately constant, attributed to sufficient contact of inner active materials.
claims.quote: the total gas generation increases from 141.3 L to roughly 190 L and maintains constant. It is deduced that the inner active materials have experienced sufficient contact once the pre-load force exceeded 2 kN, leading to the much more gas generation before the safety vent open.

claims.claim: NCA cells reach thermal runaway at a lower onset temperature (average 128.9°C) than NMC811 cells (average 130.6°C) under ARC thermal abuse testing in nitrogen atmosphere.
claims.quote: the average onset temperature fund for NCA is 128.9 ◦C while 130.6 ◦C for NMC811 cells.

claims.claim: Under ARC thermal abuse in nitrogen atmosphere, NCA cells reach an average maximum temperature of 654.3°C while NMC811 cells reach an average maximum temperature of 670.3°C during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the NCA cells have reached an average maximum temperature of 654.3 ◦C. ◦C while the NMC811 cells are 670.3 ◦C.

claims.claim: Under inert nitrogen conditions, NMC811 cells produce a higher fraction of CO2 than CO, and NMC811 cells release 66% more hydrocarbons than NCA cells during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The gas analysis indicates that NMC811 cells produce a higher fraction of CO2 than CO. Additionally, NMC811 cells release 66 % more hydrocarbons than NCA cells.

claims.claim: Prior to thermal runaway, CO2 is the main gas generated for both NMC811 and NCA chemistries; for NMC811, CO and CO2 generated prior to TR increase progressively, whereas for NCA there is no such progressive increase and gases are ejected more abruptly once TR appears.
claims.quote: Prior to TR, CO2 is the main gas that is generated for both cases. For the NMC811 LIB, the amount of CO and CO2 generated prior to reaching TR stage increases progressively. On the other hand, in NCA cells there is no such increase, and once TR appears, a more abrupt ejection of gases takes place.

claims.claim: The model assumes a fixed venting pressure threshold of 20 bars and does not account for mechanical deformation, which may contribute to discrepancies between experimental and predicted venting temperatures.
claims.quote: the model used here does not account for mechanical deformation and assumes a fixed venting pressure threshold of 20 bars.

claims.claim: LiFePO4 batteries with 100% SOC produce multiple violent jet flames during thermal runaway, while batteries with 0% SOC produce only a weak flame.
claims.quote: These studies revealed that the thermal runaway process of a battery with 0 % SOC only produces a weak flame, and the thermal runaway of a battery with 100 % SOC occurs earlier with a severe jet flame.

claims.claim: Naturally aged LiFePO4 batteries (manufactured in 2017) undergo thermal runaway earlier and produce greater amounts of smoke compared to new batteries (manufactured in 2022) under the same SOC conditions.
claims.quote: Compared with that of the new batteries (battery A and battery B), the thermal runaway of the aged batteries (battery C and battery D) occurs earlier, especially at the 0 % SOC state. In the combustion stage, the amount of smoke generated by aged batteries is greater under the same SOC conditions

claims.claim: The average mass loss during thermal runaway is approximately 110 g for LiFePO4 batteries at 100% SOC and approximately 92 g for batteries at 0% SOC.
claims.quote: The average mass losses of the batteries with 100 % and 0 % SOC are ~110 g and ~92 g, respectively. This can be attributed to the decrease in the SOC, which can relieve the chemical reaction process of the battery and cause less gas release.

claims.claim: Battery soot from LiFePO4 thermal runaway contains C, O, Li, F, P, and Fe, with F and P identified as corrosive substances that cause irritation and harm to the skin, eyes, and respiratory system.
claims.quote: The elements in battery soot include C, O, Li, F, P, and Fe... A particular concern is the presence of F and P, which are corrosive substances known to cause irritation and harm to the skin, eyes, and respiratory system

claims.claim: In the fourth stage of thermal runaway (fire extinction), gas composition released by Battery A increases by one order of magnitude compared to the third stage, corresponding to pyrolysis products of residual carbon materials.
claims.quote: In the fourth stage, the gas composition released by battery A increases by one order of magnitude, which corresponds to the pyrolysis products of residual carbon materials.

claims.claim: Battery soot particles exhibit a typical core-shell structure where carbon layers near the core are amorphous and outer layers show a degree of graphitization, indicating a more ordered carbon structure within the shell.
claims.quote: the carbon layers appear to be amorphous near the core of the battery soot. The outer layers of soot particles present a certain degree of graphitization, indicating a more ordered carbon structure within the shell. This observation is consistent with the typical core–shell structure of soot samples in hydrocarbon combustion

claims.claim: The oxygen content in battery soot increases from the first jet flame to the second jet flame: for Battery A from 5.41% to 6.13%, and for Battery C from 4.88% to 7.9%.
claims.quote: The oxygen contents in the soot generated from Batteries A and C in the first jet flame are 5.41 % and 4.88 %, respectively. These values are lower than those observed for the second jet flame (6.13 % and 7.9 %, respectively).

claims.claim: Soot from aged LiFePO4 batteries (Battery C, manufactured 2017) contains O–C=O and π bonds in addition to C–C, C–O, and O–H bonds, whereas soot from new batteries (Battery A, manufactured 2022) contains only C–C and C–O bonds on the surface.
claims.quote: The results indicate that the soot structures in Battery A and Battery C primarily contain C–C, C–O, and O–H bonds, but the soot in Battery C also includes a small number of O–C––O and π bonds.

claims.claim: Accumulation of C1–C4 gases in indoor or confined settings during the late stage of battery thermal runaway may lower oxygen levels, increasing the risk of asphyxiation and hypoxia.
claims.quote: In indoor or confined settings, C1-C4 gas accumulation may lower oxygen levels, increasing the risk of asphyxiation and hypoxia. Hence, ensuring proper ventilation during the late stage of battery thermal runaway is crucial for preventing fire ignition accidents.

claims.claim: As the SOC of NCM622 batteries increases, the internal pressure and mass loss notably rise during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The results indicate that as the state of charge increases, the internal pressure and mass loss notable rises.

claims.claim: A higher state-of-charge results in a shorter time for the battery to reach maximum temperature during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: A higher state-of-charge brings shorter time for the battery to reach maximum temperature.

claims.claim: The maximum internal pressure occurs before the peak temperature during thermal runaway, with the time interval between maximum pressure and peak temperature ranging from 20.4 s to 288.5 s depending on SOC.
claims.quote: The maximum pressure commences 20.4 (cid:0) 288.5 s prior to the moment when peak temperature occurs.

claims.claim: A positive correlation exists between internal pressure and mass loss rate during the eruption process of lithium-ion batteries undergoing thermal runaway.
claims.quote: A positive correlation has been identified between internal pressure and the mass loss rate during the eruption process.

claims.claim: Both the venting time and the ejection duration decrease as SOC increases, with venting time decreasing linearly and ejection duration decreasing exponentially.
claims.quote: Fig. 4 illustrated the analysis of venting time and ejection duration across batteries with different SOCs, revealing a linear decrease in venting time and an exponential decline in ejection duration as SOC increases.

claims.claim: Under single-sided heating conditions, a lower heating power density leads to a higher venting temperature, shorter venting time, lower TR onset temperature, higher peak temperature, and reduced TR triggering time.
claims.quote: under single-sided heating conditions, a lower heating power density leads to a higher venting temperature, shorter venting time, lower TR onset temperature, higher peak temperature, and reduced TR triggering time.

claims.claim: Expanding the heating area is more effective in initiating internal thermal reactions and accelerating the TR process compared with simply increasing the heating power density.
claims.quote: Compared with simply increasing the heating power density, expanding the heating area is more effective in initiating internal thermal reactions and accelerating the TR process.

claims.claim: Higher heating power density results in a larger gas yield during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: higher heating power density results in a larger gas yield.

claims.claim: The average heat input of 1157.25 kJ is identified as the thermal threshold for venting (pressure relief valve opening) during thermal runaway of 280 Ah LFP batteries.
claims.quote: this study identifies an average heat input of 1157.25 kJ as the thermal threshold for venting in TR simulations of this type of cell.

claims.claim: The influence of heating power density on thermal runaway behavior primarily occurs after the activation of the pressure relief valve, with minor impact before valve opening.
claims.quote: The influence of heating power density on TR behavior primarily occurs after the activation of the pressure relief valve.

claims.claim: As thermal runaway progresses, the flammable range of the gas mixture gradually narrows, reducing explosion risk.
claims.quote: as TR progresses, the flammable range of the gas mixture gradually narrows, reducing explosion risk.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway, the hydrogen-producing reaction between PVDF and lithium terminates first, followed by olefinic reactions, with CO2 being the dominant gas species in the later stages.
claims.quote: the hydrogen-producing reaction between PVDF and lithium terminates first, followed by the olefinic reactions, with CO2 being the dominant gas species in the later stages.

claims.claim: The peak temperature in Test 2 (15 cm × 15 cm single-side heating, 2.22 W/cm²) reached 346°C, which is significantly lower than the 394°C observed in Test 1 (10 cm × 10 cm single-side heating, 5 W/cm²).
claims.quote: the peak temperature in Test 2 reached 346°C, which is significantly lower than the 394°C observed in Test 1.

claims.claim: The gas volumes generated under the four test conditions follow the order Test 1 (152.28 L) > Test 2 (148.13 L) > Test 3 (138.46 L) > Test 4 (129.04 L), with higher heating power density leading to greater gas generation.
claims.quote: the gas volumes for Tests 1 to 4 are 152.28 L, 148.13 L, 138.46 L, and 129.04 L, respectively. A higher unilateral heating power density leads to increased gas production.

claims.claim: Under single-sided heating conditions, a lower heating power density results in the generation of relatively safer gaseous products during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: under single-sided heating conditions, a lower heating power density results in the generation of relatively safer gaseous products during TR.

claims.claim: Insufficient HFC-227ea increases maximum explosion pressure (Pmax) by 12.43%, 9.77%, and 1.43% under equivalence ratios of 0.7, 1.0, and 1.3, respectively.
claims.quote: a small amount of HFC-227ea increases Pmax by 12.43 %, 9.77 %, and 1.43 % under three representative equivalence ratios, respectively.

claims.claim: The explosion enhancement effect of insufficient HFC-227ea is more pronounced under BVG-deficient (fuel-lean) conditions, with the range of HFC-227ea concentration that triggers enhancement expanding significantly at equivalence ratio of 0.7.
claims.quote: the range of XHFC-227ea that triggers this phenomenon expands significantly at ϕ = 0.7, suggesting that the enhancement is more pronounced under BVG-deficient conditions.

claims.claim: When the volumetric ratios of CO2 to HFC-227ea are 5:5 and 7:3, the explosion enhancement effect is reduced by 37% and 49%, respectively.
claims.quote: When the volumetric ratios of CO2 to HFC-227ea are 5:5 and 7:3, the explosion enhancement effect is reduced by 37 % and 49 %, respectively.

claims.claim: HFC-227ea significantly increases the total quantity of burned gas under all equivalence ratios, with the most pronounced enhancement observed at equivalence ratio of 0.7, and this increase in total burned-gas quantity is identified as the dominant factor in explosion pressure enhancement.
claims.quote: HFC-227ea significantly increases the total quantity of burned gas under all equivalence ratios, with the most pronounced enhancement observed at ϕ = 0.7. In contrast, the increments induced by the M1 and M2 schemes are significantly smaller, indicating that CO2 addition can mitigate the increase in total burned-gas quantity.

claims.claim: Under fuel-rich conditions at equivalence ratio of 1.3, even when HF concentration from HFC-227ea suppression is diluted from the experimental chamber (0.02 m3) to a 10 m3 space, the peak HF value still exceeds the IDLH limit of 30 ppm, reaching 35 ppm.
claims.quote: Even when the observed HF concentration is diluted from the experimental chamber (0.02 m3) to a 10 m3 space, its peak value still exceeds the IDLH limit reaching 35 ppm (ϕ = 1.3).

claims.claim: LFP batteries charged at 1.0C and heated to 100% SOC exhibit the highest gas hazard, with a flame temperature of 2261.6 K and a laminar burning velocity of 73.2 cm/s.
claims.quote: during charging at 1.0C and heating, the gas generated when the battery reaches 100 % SOC poses the highest hazard, with a flame temperature reaching 2262 K and a laminar burning velocity of up to 73.2 cm⋅s(cid:0) 1

claims.claim: At an equivalence ratio of 1.0 with 5% DMMP added, flame temperature decreases by 32.0%, laminar burning velocity decreases by 97.4%, and net heat release decreases by 99.7%.
claims.quote: At an equivalence ratio of 1.0, with 5 % dimethyl methylphosphonate (DMMP) added, the flame temperature decreases by 32.0 %, the laminar burning velocity decreases by 97.4 %, and the net heat release decreases by 99.7 %.

claims.claim: As the proportion of DMC in the combustible gas mixture increases, both flame temperature and net heat release decrease.
claims.quote: As the proportion of DMC in the combustible gas mixture increases, both the flame temperature and net heat release decrease.

claims.claim: Compared to H2O and Novec-1230, DMMP demonstrates superior suppression effects on LFP battery fires; DMMP reduces laminar burning velocity by 97%, Novec-1230 by 92%, and H2O by only 20%.
claims.quote: DMMP exhibits better fire extinguishing performance in LFP batteries than Novec-1230 and H2O. The laminar burning velocity is reduced by 97 % for DMMP, 92 % for Novec-1230, and only 20 % for H2O.

claims.claim: The H radical is the most significant radical promoting temperature rise, while the HO2 radical plays the most prominent role in inhibiting temperature increase during LFP battery gas combustion.
claims.quote: The most significant radical promoting temperature rise is the H radical, while the most significant radical suppressing temperature rise is the HO2 radical.

claims.claim: When SOC is less than about 25%, inert gas CO2 dominates the BVG composition, exceeding 60%.
claims.quote: when SOC is less than about 25%, the inert gas CO 2 dominates, exceeding 60%.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, combustible gas components in BVG increase and gradually remain stable when SOC exceeds 75%, with CO proportion reaching about 50% and H2 about 25%.
claims.quote: With increasing SOC, the combustible gas components increase and gradually remain stable when SOC exceeds 75%. Among them, the proportion of CO is the highest, about 50%, followed by H 2 , about 25%, while the inert gas decreases to about 20%.

claims.claim: The BVG explosion limits exhibit a Z-shaped curve under all SOC conditions, demonstrating the dominance of H2 in the explosion response.
claims.quote: the BVG explosion limits exhibit the Z-shaped curve. With increasing SOC, the explosion limit gradually shifts to the low-temperature regime at higher pressures.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, the proportion of C2H4 in BVG increases, and the nonlinear characteristics of the third explosion limit become more prominent, causing the overall explosion temperature to move to the low temperature region.
claims.quote: With increasing SOC, the proportion of C 2 H 4 in the BVG increases, and the non-linear characteristics of the third explosion limit become more prominent. In addition, the high-pressure chemical reaction of C 2 H 4 has a strong effect, so the overall explosion temperature moves to the low temperature region, which means that the BVG is more likely to cause an explosion.

claims.claim: At the third explosion limit, the pressure needed to trigger explosion increases in the order: C2H4, CH4, H2, and CO, with C2H4 having the lowest explosion pressure due to stronger low-temperature chemical chain reactions of hydrocarbons compared to hydrogen at high-pressure conditions.
claims.quote: at the third explosion limit, the pressure needed to trigger explosion in order from low to high is C 2 H 4 , CH 4 , H 2 , and CO. The low temperature chemical chain reaction of hydrocarbons is stronger than hydrogen at high-pressure condition, leading to the fact that C 2 H 4 has the lowest explosion pressure.

claims.claim: HF emissions production is consistently higher during the safety venting stage than during thermal runaway across all tested conditions.
claims.quote: HF emissions production is consistently higher during the safety venting stage than during thermal runaway. This is because the primary source of HF is electrolyte decomposition, which occurs at relatively low temperatures.

claims.claim: At 0% SOC and heating flux values of 27 and 36 kW/m2, the battery only exhibited safety venting fire without progressing to thermal runaway; thermal runaway was only triggered at 0% SOC when a higher heating flux of 53 kW/m2 was applied.
claims.quote: A special case at 0% SOC was observed that at applied heating flux values of 27 and 36 kW/m2, the 0% SOC battery only exhibited safety venting fire without progressing to thermal runaway. Thermal runaway was eventually observed at 0% SOC when a higher heating flux (53 kW/m2) was applied.

claims.claim: At a constant heat flux, the thermal runaway delay time with respect to safety venting decreases with increasing SOC because higher SOC levels contain more electrochemical energy, which accelerates the reactions leading to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: At a constant heat flux, the thermal runaway delay time with respect to safety venting decreases with increasing SOC. This is because higher SOC levels contain more electrochemical energy, which accelerates the reactions leading to thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The peak HF concentration measured by in situ LAS reached several percent in mole fraction, which is two orders of magnitude higher than values reported in existing literature, likely due to high temporal resolution and elimination of sampling line losses.
claims.quote: The peak concentration of HF emissions reached several percent in mole fraction, which is two orders of magnitude higher than values reported in existing literature [10]. This discrepancy is likely due to the high temporal resolution of LAS measurements, which accurately captures transient emission peaks, as well as the direct in situ measurement approach, which eliminates potential gas losses caused by adsorption or surface reaction inside the sampling lines.

claims.claim: Lower external heating flux levels lead to a decrease in total HF generation because intermediate fluoridated gas species such as PF5 and POF3 generated from electrolyte decomposition are emitted before their subsequent hydrolysis reactions that require higher temperatures.
claims.quote: lower external heating flux levels would lead to a decrease in total HF generation likely because the intermediate fluoridated gas species such as PF5, POF3, generated from electrolyte decomposition reactions (1) and (3) were emitted before their subsequent hydrolysis reactions (2) and (4) which require a higher temperature.

claims.claim: Above a 'knee point' temperature of approximately 215°C, the rate of thermal decomposition of 1 M LiPF6+EC/DMC=1:1 v:v electrolyte abruptly increases and the amount of released gas quickly accumulates.
claims.quote: There is also an explicit 'knee point' at around 215 °C, above which the rate of thermal decomposition abruptly increases and the amount of released gas quickly accumulates.

claims.claim: The total volume of gas released from 10 mL of 1 M LiPF6+EC/DMC=1:1 v:v electrolyte at 300°C increases with pyrolysis duration: 1397.9 mL at 20 min, 1755.7 mL at 40 min, and 2402.2 mL at 60 min.
claims.quote: At the highest temperature of 300 °C in this experiment, the total gas volume are 1397.9 mL, 1755.7 mL, and 2402.2 mL for 20, 40, and 60-min pyrolysis durations, respectively.

claims.claim: The volume concentration of CO2 is approximately 400 times larger than that of CO during thermal decomposition of 1 M LiPF6+EC/DMC=1:1 v:v electrolyte.
claims.quote: the volume concentration of CO2 is about 400 times larger than CO.

claims.claim: With increasing oxygen concentration during thermal decomposition of electrolyte at 230°C for 60 min, CO concentration first increases then decreases sharply, while CO2 concentration increases monotonically.
claims.quote: while the concentration of CO2 increases with the concentration of oxygen in the background gas, the concentration of CO would increase first and then decrease sharply.

claims.claim: The volume concentration of C2H4O2 decreases with prolonged pyrolysis duration, decreasing by approximately 250–380 ppmv from 20 to 40 min and by only 15–90 ppmv from 40 to 60 min.
claims.quote: the volume concentration of C2H4O2, which is another neurotoxic gas, decreases by around 250 – 380 ppmv from 20 to 40 min for different temperatures, while they only decrease by around 15–90 ppmv from 40 to 60 min.

claims.claim: For 42 Ah NCM111 Li-ion prismatic cells, the sum of CO2, H2, C2H4, CO, and CH4 accounted for more than 90% of the vented gases during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: CO2, H2, C2H4, CO, and CH4 accounted for large proportions, and their sum exceeded 90%

claims.claim: The proportion of CO in the vented gas at 100% SOC was three times that at 30% SOC during thermal runaway of NCM111 cells.
claims.quote: the proportion of CO increased signiﬁcantly under higher SOC conditions, which at 100% SOC was three times of that at 30% SOC

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, the maximum explosion pressure (Pmax) and maximum pressure rate ((dP/dt)max) both increased for NCM111 cell vented gas mixtures ignited in a 22 L explosion-proof sphere.
claims.quote: With the rise of SOC, the maximum explosion pressure (Pmax) and the maximum pressure rate ((dP/dt)max) also increased, indicating that more energy was released more rapidly in the form of shockwaves during the TR of Li-ion cells at higher SOC

claims.claim: Both LEL and UEL of the vented gas mixture first decreased and then slightly increased with ascending SOC; LEL was lowest at 50% SOC and UEL was highest at 30% SOC.
claims.quote: the change trends of LEL and UEL were similar with the ascending of SOC, and both of them ﬁrst decreased and then slightly increased. Since the LEL was the lowest at 50% SOC, the gas explosion was most likely to occur during the TR of the Li-ion cells under this condition. Additionally, the UEL was the highest at 30% SOC.

claims.claim: The measured maximum explosion pressures (Pmax) at all SOCs were lower than the corresponding CJ detonation pressures (Pcj), indicating that the detonation was quasi-detonation rather than overdriven detonation.
claims.quote: the Pmax values measured at different SOCs were all lower than the corresponding Pcj. This demonstrates that the detonation was not overdriven but a quasi-detonation at this time

claims.claim: CuO decoration decreases the band gap of the InSe monolayer from 1.938 eV to 0.831 eV, enhancing electronic sensitivity.
claims.quote: CuO decoration decreases the InSe band gap from 1.938 eV to 0.831 eV, enhancing electronic sensitivity.

claims.claim: Compared to pristine InSe, CuO decoration improves adsorption of CO, C2H2, and C2H4 by 130.1%, 76.9%, and 75.7%, respectively.
claims.quote: Adsorption of CO, C₂H₂, and C₂H₄ improves by 130.1 %, 76.9 %, and 75.7 %, respectively, compared to pristine InSe.

claims.claim: The adsorption of CO, C2H2, C2H4, and CO2 on the pristine InSe monolayer is physical adsorption, as indicated by minimal adsorption energy and charge transfer with no significant chemical bond formation.
claims.quote: The adsorption energy and charge transfer of the material during gas adsorption are minimal, with no significant chemical bond formation or electron transfer, indicating physical adsorption.

claims.claim: At 298 K, the desorption times of CO, C2H2, and C2H4 on CuO-InSe are extremely long (6.594×10^12 s, 4.347×10^10 s, and 1.571×10^11 s, respectively), while the desorption time of CO2 is extremely short (1.062×10^-9 s), indicating CuO-InSe is suitable as an adsorbent for CO, C2H2, and C2H4 but functions as a rapid-response sensor for CO2.
claims.quote: the resolution times of CO, C2H2, and C2H4 are extremely long at 298 K, indicating that the CuO-InSe material can be used as an adsorbent for CO, C2H2, and C2H4 thermal runaway failure gases... For the CO2 adsorption system, the desorption times at 298 K, 398 K, and 498 K are extremely short, demonstrating that although the CuO-InSe material has a low capacity for CO2 adsorption, it reacts rapidly and may be used as a gas sensor to detect thermal runaway failure in lithium batteries early.

claims.claim: The desorption times of CO, C2H2, and C2H4 on CuO-InSe decrease rapidly with increasing ambient temperature, and different gases can be separated by controlling the ambient temperature.
claims.quote: the desorption times of the three gases decreased rapidly with increasing ambient temperature. Different gases can be separated by managing the ambient temperature, which is useful for evaluating the thermal runaway failure stage of lithium batteries.

claims.claim: As CO partial pressure increases, the band gap of CuO-InSe shows an overall decreasing trend, with the most pronounced decrease occurring during the T2 fault initiation stage (350 K to 475 K).
claims.quote: Eg shows an overall decreasing trend as the partial pressure of CO gas increases, and the decrease is most obvious in the T2 period, which may be due to the chemical reaction occurring at this stage that helps to improve the conductivity at the electrode-electrolyte interface.

claims.claim: NMC-derived TR particulates had a higher count mean diameter (173.7 ± 7.40 nm) and higher particle concentration (401.1 ± 30.1 mg/m3) compared to LFP-derived TR particulates (CMD: 137.2 ± 5.65 nm; concentration: 356.3 ± 23.2 mg/m3).
claims.quote: The CMD (Fig. 2A) of LFP- and NMC-derived TR particulates were 137.2 ± 5.65 and 173.7 ± 7.40 nm, respectively. Particle concentrations of LFP- and NMC-derived TR particulates were 356.3 ± 23.2 and 401.1 ± 30.1 mg/m3

claims.claim: Manganese (Mn) was found exclusively in NMC-derived TR particulate filter extracts and was not detected in LFP-derived TR-PM, irrespective of SOC.
claims.quote: Mn was found exclusively in NMC-derived filter extracts.

claims.claim: Treatment with the 5-year cumulative exposure dose of NMC-derived TR-PM, but not the 1-year dose, led to significantly elevated ROS production in SAEC after 24 h exposure (1.22 ± 0.06-fold change; p = 0.0234).
claims.quote: treatment with a 5-year cumulative exposure dose (1.22 ± 0.06-fold change; p = 0.0234), but not a 1-year dose (1.08 ± 0.03-fold change; p = 0.8031), of NMC-derived TR-PM led to an elevated production of ROS compared to control following a 24 h exposure

claims.claim: LFP-derived TR-PM did not induce elevated ROS production relative to control at either the 1-year or 5-year dose, or at either 24 h or 7-day time points.
claims.quote: LFP-derived TR-PM did not induce higher levels of ROS production, relative to control, at either dose (i.e., 1-year vs. 5-year) or either time point (i.e., 24 h vs. 7-day)

claims.claim: When TR-PM particulates were dispersed in water alone, particle size and polydispersity index increased significantly, promoting agglomerate formation, whereas dispersion in cell culture media reduced particle size and PI, promoting even dispersion.
claims.quote: the hydrodynamic diameter and polydispersity index (PI) of both LFP and NMC-derived TR-PM increased significantly when dispersed in UP-H2O, while the diameter and PI were reduced drastically for LFP TR-PM in cell culture medium. These findings suggest that TR-PM dispersed in water alone promotes the formation of agglomerates or particle clusters, while cell culture media containing stabilization proteins promotes even dispersion of the particulates.

claims.claim: The number of identified combustion organic products (COPs) from 18,650 LIBs increased with SOC from 0% to 100%, but decreased when SOC increased further from 100% to 150% (overcharge).
claims.quote: the NIs of COPs from those four kinds of LIBs is dependent strongly on the batteries' SOC. Also, it is following the trend that the NIs increased with the SOC increasing from 0% to 100%, but reduced with the SOC continuously increasing from 100% to 150% (overcharge).

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the level I and II toxic combustion organic products appeared 15 times across all battery types tested, which was more than at any other SOC tested (50%: 9 times, 150%: 7 times, 0%: 7 times), making 100% SOC the most dangerous state of charge in terms of toxicity.
claims.quote: the level I and II toxic COPs appeared 15 times, 9 times, 7 times and 7 times at SOC of 100%, 50%, 150% and 0% respectively. At 100% SOC... the 100% SOC is the most dangerous state of charge in terms of toxicity and hazards.

claims.claim: CO concentration emitted from 10 Ah pouch cell combustion (LMO: 18,700 ppm; NMC: 14,000 ppm) was significantly higher than from 18,650 cells (LMO: 1200 ppm; NMC: 616 ppm), indicating that higher capacity cells generate much more CO during thermal runaway combustion.
claims.quote: burning of the 10 A h pouch cell produced more than 10,000 ppm CO, as shown in Table 4, which is signiﬁcantly higher than the concentration that can cause serious sickness, and even death (1000–2000 ppm in 1–2 min)... the CCO of 10 A h cell was much higher than 18,650 cell, which indicated that in a limit space environment, cell with higher capacity would generate much more CO during thermal runaway combustion. The CCO emitted increased 15–22 times when the capacity increased only about 3 times.

claims.claim: For 100% SOC NMC LIBs, CO concentration increased sharply with increasing capacity (616, 1100, 3900, 14,000, and 45,500 ppm for 2, 3, 6.5, 10, and 30 Ah respectively), while concentrations of toxic organic COPs (Naphthalene, Benzene, Pentadiene, Styrene, Toluene, Xylene, Indene) remained at the level of several to tens of ppm without obvious increase.
claims.quote: the CCO increase sharply with increasing capacities, which are 616, 1100, 3900, 14,000 and 45,500 ppm when the capacities are from 2, 3, 6.5, 10 A h to 30 A h, respectively. The concentrations of COPs Naphthalene, Benzene, Pentadiene, Styrene, Toluene, Xylene and Indene do not show obvious increase compared to CO, but keep at the level of several to decades ppm.

claims.claim: LFP 18,650 cell showed a milder combustion process compared to LMO, NMC, and LCO cells, with only a weak flame and small amount of smoke observed after 3 min 12 s of heat treatment, which then extinguished spontaneously, while LMO exploded violently and LCO showed severe combustion.
claims.quote: Only LFP showed a relatively milder combustion process, in which a weak ﬂame with small amount of smog was observed after heat treated for 3 min 12 s, and then was extinct spontaneously... These tests indicate that the LFP does show signiﬁcantly enhanced safety in view of explosion.

claims.claim: The heating coil method triggered thermal runaway most quickly at 225 s, with a pre-runaway peak temperature of 154.47°C, which was 48.94°C and 38.06°C higher than the temperatures triggered by the heating rod and plate, respectively.
claims.quote: thermal runaway is triggered after 225 s of heating with the coil, reaching 140.07 C higher than the temperatures triggered by the rod and plate, respectively. Triggering times are reduced by 34.00 s and 57.79 s, respectively.

claims.claim: The peak temperature after thermal runaway was lowest for the heating coil (326.03°C), intermediate for the heating plate (460.35°C), and highest for the heating rod (508.67°C).
claims.quote: The peak temperature data indicate the following order from lowest to highest: heating coil (326.03 C), heating plate (460.35 C), and heating rod (508.67 C).

claims.claim: Under heating coil conditions, the peak chamber pressure was the highest at 430.43 kPa despite the lowest ambient temperature, indicating that higher gas generation rate is the primary driver of pressure increase.
claims.quote: under heating coil conditions, the ambient temperature is the lowest, yet the corresponding peak pressure is the highest at 430.43 kPa. This indicates that a higher gas generation rate is the primary driver of the pressure increase.

claims.claim: The heating coil method produced the highest peak gas generation at 7.72 mol, significantly exceeding the heating plate and rod methods, with all methods stabilizing between 1.7 and 2 mol after the peak.
claims.quote: The heating coil method shows the highest peak gas generation at 7.72 mol, significantly exceeding the heating plate and rod methods. Comparing peak gas generation from different heating methods with the corresponding peak pressure, peak temperature, and ambient temperature in Fig. 6 reveals a clear relationship. Peak pressure and ambient temperature rise with increased gas generation, while peak temperature after thermal runaway decreases.

claims.claim: The heating rod method produced the widest flammable concentration range (51.11%) and the lowest lower flammability limit (7.97%) among the three heating methods, making it the most hazardous in terms of explosion risk.
claims.quote: the flammable concentration ranges from largest to smallest for the three heating methods is: heating rod (51.11 %), heating coil (49.82 %), and heating plate (46.94 %). Furthermore, the lower flammability limit is the lowest for the heating rod (7.97 %), followed by the heating plate (8.26 %) and the heating coil (8.88 %).

claims.claim: The concentration of H2 in the vent gas was inversely related to heating efficiency, with the descending order being heating rod (25.06%), heating plate (24.33%), and heating coil (23.61%), attributed to rapid oxidation of reactants at higher heating efficiencies leaving insufficient reactants to produce H2.
claims.quote: the descending order of H2 gas concentrations from high to low is heating rod (25.06 %), heating plate (24.33 %), and heating coil (23.61 %). This difference may be due to the rapid oxidation of reactants at higher heating efficiencies, causing insufficient reactants to produce H2.

claims.claim: The CO2 concentration in vent gas was highest for the heating coil (27.73%), followed by the heating plate (24.6%) and the heating rod (18.08%), with larger heating areas promoting faster temperature increases that accelerate CO2 production from SEI and electrolyte decomposition.
claims.quote: the descending order of CO2 gas concentrations is as follows: heating coil (27.73 %), heating plate (24.6 %), and heating rod (18.08 %). Notably, the CO2 gas concentration with heating rod heating is significantly lower than the other two heating methods.

claims.claim: For cylindrical-type battery cells, second venting accompanied by thermal runaway was observed at 24.2 s after the first venting was confirmed.
claims.quote: In the case of cylindrical- and pouch-type battery cells, second venting accompanied by thermal runaway was observed at 24.2 s and 67 s, respectively, after the first venting was confirmed

claims.claim: For pouch-type battery cells, second venting accompanied by thermal runaway was observed at 67 s after the first venting was confirmed.
claims.quote: In the case of cylindrical- and pouch-type battery cells, second venting accompanied by thermal runaway was observed at 24.2 s and 67 s, respectively, after the first venting was confirmed

claims.claim: With cooling fan operation, DMC gas detection time at the suction pipe was 0.558 s, compared to 2.968 s without fan operation, representing an 81.18% reduction in detection time.
claims.quote: The detection times of DMC gas at the suction pipe were 2.968 s and 0.558 s, respectively, indicating a significant difference of 81.18 %. This difference is due to the forced convection effect of the cooling fan, which increased the advection rate of DMC gas toward the suction pipe.

claims.claim: During the early stage of first venting, the cooling fan did not significantly affect gas flow behavior because the venting momentum was significantly larger than the flow field induced by the cooling fan.
claims.quote: operating the cooling fan rarely affects DMC gas flows during the early venting stage because the venting momentum is significantly larger than the flow field induced by the cooling fan.

claims.claim: Increasing the cooling fan intensity by 30% yielded a 22.28% improvement in detection time for left-side venting and a 19.57% improvement for right-side venting when venting occurred in the last battery cell.
claims.quote: Further increasing the fan intensity by 30 % yielded a 22.28 % improvement for left-side venting and a 19.57 % improvement for right-side venting.

claims.claim: The detection time difference between left-side and right-side venting was greatest for the middle battery cell, reaching 4.021 × 10−1 s, and smallest for the last battery cell.
claims.quote: The time difference was most notable in the middle cells, depending on the venting direction, and reached 4.021 × 10−1 s. In contrast, the difference at the last battery cell was the smallest.

claims.claim: When the cooling fan operated at 30% increased intensity (6.635 × 10−3 kg/s), detection was achieved within 1000 s across all venting locations in the last battery cell cases, and the detection time difference between left- and right-side venting increased with fan intensity.
claims.quote: when the fan operated at a 30 % increased intensity, corresponding to 6.635 × 10−3 kg/s, venting was detected within 1000 s in all cases. It was suggested that the optimal detection performance could be achieved under the current sensor configuration when the cooling fan operated at a higher intensity, and the detection time difference between left- and right-side venting increased with the fan intensity.

claims.claim: Nano SiAG felt achieved the highest temperature difference (ΔTTIM 1) of 578°C between the triggered cell and the adjacent cell, indicating the strongest thermal runaway propagation mitigation among the tested materials.
claims.quote: the ΔTTIM 1 values for each experimental group are as follows: Nano SiAG felt group (578 °C), mica plate group (541 °C), SiAG pre-oxidized fiber group (523 °C), aluminosilicate ceramic fiber group (−152 °C), and the group without thermal insulation material (−767 °C).

claims.claim: Aluminosilicate ceramic fiber insulation did not prevent thermal runaway propagation to all cells, but significantly reduced the propagation rate, extending jet fire time intervals from 53 s, 30 s, 15 s (no insulation) to 130 s, 120 s, 37 s respectively.
claims.quote: in the group without insulation material, the time intervals for jet fire of Cells 2–4 were 53 s, 30 s, and 15 s, respectively, while in the experimental group using aluminosilicate ceramic fiber insulation material, the corresponding time intervals were extended to 130 s, 120 s, and 37 s.

claims.claim: Aluminosilicate ceramic fiber exhibited the highest mass loss among TIMs, reaching 33.18% at 900°C with a continuous non-stepped weight loss profile, indicative of poor thermal stability in high-temperature environments.
claims.quote: aluminosilicate ceramic fiber exhibited a continuous, non-stepped weight loss profile, reaching a maximum weight loss of 33.18 % at 900 °C, indicative of poor thermal stability in high-temperature environments.

claims.claim: The elevated NO2 peak observed specifically in the mica plate group is attributed to the oxidative thermal decomposition of the nitrogen-based adhesive in the mica binder, not to electrochemical reactions within the cell.
claims.quote: the significantly elevated NO2 peak observed specifically in the Mica plate group is attributed to the oxidative thermal decomposition of the nitrogen-based adhesive used in the mica binder, rather than electrochemical reactions within the cell.

claims.claim: Aluminosilicate ceramic fiber experienced the most significant structural shrinkage after TRP testing, with a 69% contour area shrinkage rate, which led to thermal insulation failure.
claims.quote: the most significant lateral shrinkage and uneven longitudinal change for the aluminosilicate ceramic fiber group (69 % contour area shrinkage rate). The significant shrinkage of the aluminosilicate ceramic fiber group led to thermal insulation failure.

claims.claim: Nano SiAG felt exhibited minimal final mass loss of only 2.51% at 900°C in TGA tests, suggesting good thermal stability for medium-to-high temperature insulation applications.
claims.quote: Nano SiAG felt also exhibited minimal final weight loss, with a reduction of only 2.51 % at 900 °C, suggesting that it performs well as an insulating material in medium-to high-temperature ranges.

claims.claim: A cell with higher internal resistance, when subjected to an internal short circuit, generates more heat via Joule heating, shortens thermal runaway incubation time, and increases the risk of propagation to adjacent cells.
claims.quote: in the event of an internal short circuit (ISC), the large current (I) flowing through a higher internal resistance (R) generates a significantly larger and more rapid burst of heat... this intense initial heat generation acts as a more potent trigger, drastically shortening the incubation time for thermal runaway and accelerating the onset of subsequent exothermic chain reactions... a cell with higher internal resistance is not only more prone to initiating TR but also transfers heat more violently to adjacent cells, thereby increasing the risk of propagation.

claims.claim: For both NCA and LFP batteries, as SOC decreases, the UFL of the BVG remains nearly constant while the LFL increases sharply, causing the UFL and LFL curves to form a peninsula shape and converge to equal values at a critical low SOC.
claims.quote: For both types of batteries, the UFL remained nearly constant as the SOC decreased, but the LFL kept nearly constant ﬁrst and then increased sharply. The UFL and LFL approached the same value as the SOC decreases. The curves of ﬂammability limits of the BVG had a phenomenon of ﬂammability peninsula with decreasing SOC.

claims.claim: At the same SOC, the LFP battery BVG has a higher LFL and lower flammability range (RF) than the NCA battery BVG, resulting in a significantly lower flammability hazard index (HF) for LFP.
claims.quote: Compared with the NCA battery, the LFL of the LFP battery's vent gas is higher, and the RF is lower, resulting in lower HF signiﬁcantly at the same SOC. This is one of the main reasons for LFP batteries being safer than other variants.

claims.claim: For a SOC range of 0% to 130%, the maximum HF of the LFP battery BVG is 381% and that of the NCA battery BVG is 519%, with LFP being 27% lower than NCA.
claims.quote: At a SOC of 0%–130%, the maximum HF of the LFP and NCA batteries' vent gas is 381% and 519%, respectively. The former is 27% lower than the latter.

claims.claim: Increasing the CO2 diluent ratio (DR) at constant SOC causes the RF of the BVG to decrease significantly; for LFP LIBs the maximum RF dropped by 8%, 20%, 35%, and 49% at DR of 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively compared with no dilution, and for NCA LIBs it dropped by 15%, 35%, 60%, and 87% at the same DR values.
claims.quote: It dropped by 8%, 20%, 35% and 49% for the LFP LIB, and by 15%, 35%, 60% and 87% for the NCA LIB, when the DR was 1, 2, 3 and 4 compared with no dilution, respectively.

claims.claim: For a SOC range of 0% to 130%, the maximum diluent ratio threshold (DRF) required to render the BVG non-flammable is 3.4 for LFP LIBs and 4.2 for NCA LIBs, with LFP being 19% lower than NCA.
claims.quote: For a SOC range of 0% to 130%, the maximum DRFs were 3.4 and 4.2 for LFP and NCA LIBs, respectively. The former is 19% lower than the latter.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature of a LiNi1/3Co1/3Mn1/3O2/graphite battery during thermal runaway increases linearly with SOC, following the relationship T_max = 617.063 * SOC + 318.799 (R² = 0.9156).
claims.quote: the approximated relationship of onset temperature and the maximum temperature to the battery SOC are calculated as and , respectively, where R2 is the goodness of fit

claims.claim: The thermal runaway onset temperature of a LiNi1/3Co1/3Mn1/3O2/graphite battery decreases linearly with increasing SOC, following the relationship T_onset = -38.042 * SOC + 179.8009 (R² = 0.9401).
claims.quote: the approximated relationship of onset temperature and the maximum temperature to the battery SOC are calculated as and , respectively, where R2 is the goodness of fit

claims.claim: At 5% SOC, the peak temperature of the LiNi1/3Co1/3Mn1/3O2/graphite battery during thermal runaway is approximately 289 °C, significantly lower than at higher SOCs.
claims.quote: the temperature of the 5% SOC battery grows moderately, as its peak temperature is only around 289 °C, which is significantly lower than the peak temperature of the same type of battery at a higher SOC

claims.claim: At 90% and 100% SOC, LiNi1/3Co1/3Mn1/3O2/graphite batteries experienced gas deflagration and particle ejection, generating 1.22 g and 2.57 g of powder respectively, while lower SOC batteries did not exhibit this behavior.
claims.quote: both the 90% and the 100% SOC batteries experienced some degree of gas deflagration and particle ejection that generated 1.22 g and 2.57 g of powders, respectively

claims.claim: The number of gas types released during thermal runaway increases with SOC: 6, 10, 15, and 25 gas types were identified at 5%, 50%, 90%, and 100% SOC respectively for LiNi1/3Co1/3Mn1/3O2/graphite batteries.
claims.quote: there are 6, 10, 15, and 25 gas types identified for the 5%, 50%, 90%, and 100% SOC batteries

claims.claim: The concentration of HF in gases released from thermally abused LiNi1/3Co1/3Mn1/3O2/graphite batteries is higher at higher SOC, indicating greater toxicity at higher SOC.
claims.quote: the concentration of HF is higher at a higher SOC due to the deeper pink colour of its corresponding detector, revealing that the gas released from a higher SOC battery is inevitably more toxic

claims.claim: For 90% and 100% SOC batteries, internal electrode assemblies were completely destroyed with electrode substances falling off and separators burnt down, whereas for 5% and 50% SOC batteries, electrode substances remained attached and separators only shrank.
claims.quote: for the 5% and 50% SOC batteries, their electrode substances are still attached to the collector surface, and the separator is not burnt down, but it rather shrinks. More seriously, for the 90% and 100% SOC batteries, their internal electrode assembly are completely destroyed, with their electrode substances fell off and their separators burnt down

claims.claim: The total mass of settleable PM released during thermal runaway of a 50 Ah NMC lithium-ion cell was 97.47 g, accounting for 11.20% of the cell mass.
claims.quote: The total mass of the settleable PM was 97.47 g, accounting for 11.20% of the cell mass

claims.claim: Nearly 45% of settleable PM by mass had a particle size less than 0.85 mm, with the median equivalent particle size (D50) being approximately 198 μm for sample a.
claims.quote: Nearly 45% of particles by mass were found to have a size less than 0.85 mm, with the median size being approximately 198 um.

claims.claim: Using a filter with a pore size below 0.85 mm would filter out more than 50% of the settleable PM released during LIB thermal runaway.
claims.quote: if a ﬁlter with a pore size below 0.85 mm was used, more than 50% of the settleable PM would be ﬁltered

claims.claim: The metallic elements in the settleable PM accounted for approximately 42.9% of the total settleable PM mass, with content varying between 30% and 50% across individual samples.
claims.quote: The metallic elements accounted for 42.9% of the total settleable PM mass... The content of metallic elements in each sample varied between 30% and 50%

claims.claim: The settleable PM contains potentially toxic elements including aluminum, lithium, fluorine, and tin, with overall mass percentages of 6.9%, 3.9%, 0.002%, and 0.001% respectively, which can be enriched thousands of times by biomagnification before entering the human body.
claims.quote: particles contain elements [47,48] such as Al, Li, F and Sn, which are potentially toxic to the human body, with mass percentages of 6.9%, 3.9%, 0.002%, and 0.001%, respectively. They can be enriched thousands of times over by biomagniﬁcation of the food chain before ﬁnally entering the human body

claims.claim: Under argon atmosphere, the peak concentration of carbon monoxide was nearly 50% lower than in air atmosphere during lithium-ion battery thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Without the support of oxygen in the air, the content of carbon monoxide was significantly reduced. The peak concentrations of carbon monoxide changed by nearly 50%.

claims.claim: The concentration of CO2 during thermal runaway in air environment (1989 ppm) was approximately 50% higher than in argon environment (961 ppm).
claims.quote: The concentration of carbon dioxide in the air environment is 1989 ppm, while in the argon environment it is 961 ppm, a drop close to 50%.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, the time required for thermal runaway decreased, more heat was generated, and the internal reaction of the battery became more intense.
claims.quote: with the increase of SOC, the shorter the time required for thermal runaway, the more heat was generated, and the more intense the internal reaction of the battery.

claims.claim: At an ambient temperature of 180 °C, the concentration of carbon monoxide was higher than at an ambient temperature of 220 °C during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the concentration of carbon monoxide was higher than that of carbon monoxide at 220 °C. But at 180 °C, the concentration of carbon monoxide was higher than that of carbon monoxide at 220 °C.

claims.claim: With decreasing electric heating power, the concentrations of both CO and CO2 decreased during lithium-ion battery thermal runaway.
claims.quote: with the weakening of the electric heating power of lithium ions, the concentration of carbon monoxide in the mixed gas tended to decrease gradually... the gas production of carbon dioxide was similar to that of carbon monoxide.

claims.claim: The risk of explosion increases with increasing state of charge (SOC) for 18650 lithium-ion batteries in hermetic space.
claims.quote: the experimental results showed that the risk of explosion upgrading with the increase of SOC

claims.claim: Before the explosion, H2 concentration reached a peak of 1628 ppm (0.1628%) and CO reached 668 ppm (0.0668%), both far below their lower flammability limits of 4.0% and 12.5% respectively, indicating H2 and CO are not the main trigger of the explosion.
claims.quote: the concentration of H2 and CO increased sharply and reached peaks of 1628ppm (0.1628%) and 668ppm (0.0668%) respectively before the explosion... The results showed that the contents of H2 and CO before the explosion are far below their LFLs (4.0% and 12.5%), indicating that H2 and CO are not the main trigger of the explosion.

claims.claim: Internal short circuit events increased the vaporization rate of electrolytes, with the amplitude and rate of voltage drop caused by internal short circuit gradually increasing with continuous overcharge.
claims.quote: the amplitude and rate of voltage drop caused by the internal short circuit gradually increased. In other words, the ability of battery to withstand overcharge gradually reduced. The degree of internal short circuit gradually increased, which manifested the battery module was approaching the thermal runaway state constantly.

claims.claim: When the overpressure in the central container reached a peak of 20 kPa, the pressure on the pressure relief plates of the adjacent container at 3 m on the opposite side reached a peak of 4.5 kPa, which may push open those plates and allow direct high-temperature or flame influence on the interior of the adjacent container.
claims.quote: When the overpressure in the container reached the peak value of 20kPa, the pressure on the 8# relief pressure plates reached the peak value of 4.5kPa. That is to say, when the setting of pressure relief plate was consistent, the PRPs at 3m on the opposite side may be pushed away by the explosion overpressure, resulting in the direct influence of high-temperature or even flame on the interior of the adjacent container.

claims.claim: In the numerical study of the ESS explosion, the area above 343 K was larger at a low altitude layer of 0.4 m than at heights of 1.5 m or 2.8 m, and the longest expansion distance of the high-temperature zone was about 8 m in the passage perpendicular to the container door.
claims.quote: The area above 343K in Fig. 10c was larger than that in Fig. 10a and 10b... The longest expansion distance of the high-temperature zone was about 8m in the passage perpendicular to the container door.

claims.claim: At 0% SOC, the maximum battery surface temperature during thermal runaway heating was only 277.8°C, and thermal runaway did not occur.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature of 0%SOC is only 277.8 ◦C, and thermal runaway will not even occur.

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, the onset time of thermal runaway advances, the onset temperature decreases significantly, the maximum temperature increases significantly, and thermal safety decreases significantly.
claims.quote: with the further increase of charge, the occurrence time of thermal runaway of the battery is gradually advanced, the occurrence temperature of thermal runaway decreases significantly, the maximum temperature increases significantly, and the thermal safety decreases significantly.

claims.claim: The maximum temperatures reached during thermal runaway were 277.8°C, 506.6°C, 550.8°C, 645.5°C, and 777.7°C for 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100% SOC batteries, respectively.
claims.quote: The highest temperature/◦C: 277.8, 506.6, 550.8, 645.5, 777.7

claims.claim: With increasing SOC, the lower explosion limit (LEL) of thermal runaway exhaust gas shows the same trend as alkane content, increasing first and then decreasing, while the upper explosion limit (UEL) shows the same trend as unsaturated hydrocarbon content, decreasing first and then increasing.
claims.quote: with the increase of SOC, the change of lower explosion limit (LEL) shows the same trend as that in alkanes content, which increases and then decreases; while the change of upper explosion limit (UEL) shows the same trend as that in unsaturated hydrocarbons content, which decreases and then increases.

claims.claim: The 50% SOC battery had the highest LEL at 10.98% and the lowest UEL at 40.06%, indicating the lowest exhausted gas combustion explosion danger among the tested SOC groups.
claims.quote: the lower explosion limit of 50% SOC battery pyrolysis gas is the highest, at 10.98%, and the upper explosion limit is the lowest, at 40.06%.

claims.claim: For a charged (4.2 V) 18650 Sony LiCoO2 Li-ion battery tested in a VSP2 adiabatic calorimeter without a buffer tank, the maximum temperature (Tmax) and maximum pressure (Pmax) during thermal runaway reached 903.0 °C and 1565.9 psig, respectively, resulting in a thermal explosion with a heat of reaction of 26.2 kJ.
claims.quote: The Tmax and Pmax of the charged Li-ion battery during the runaway reaction reach 903.0 ◦C and 1565.9 psig (pound-force per square inch gauge), respectively. This result leads to a thermal explosion, and the heat of reaction is 26.2 kJ.

claims.claim: The heat of reaction for charged (4.2 V) 18650 LiCoO2 Li-ion batteries as determined by VSP2 adiabatic experiments was 18.55 ± 0.55 kJ, which is much higher than for uncharged (3.7 V) batteries.
claims.quote: The heat of reaction for the charged Li-ion batteries as determined by VSP2 experiments was 18.55 ± 0.55 kJ, which is much higher than for the uncharged batteries.

claims.claim: The porous separator breaks down at temperatures above 120 °C and then induces an internal short circuit in Li-ion batteries.
claims.quote: the porous separator breaks down at temperatures above 120 ◦C and then induces an internal short circuit

claims.claim: The thermal reactive hazards of charged (4.2 V) Li-ion batteries exponentially increased at approximately 200 °C, while uncharged (3.7 V) batteries did not exhibit such a sharp trend.
claims.quote: The thermal reactive hazards of the charged Li-ion batteries exponentially increased at approximately 200 ◦C, while the uncharged batteries did not have such a sharp trend.

claims.claim: The lowest initial exothermic temperature (T0) among charged 18650 LiCoO2 batteries tested was that of the Samsung battery at 116.5 °C, indicating the earliest onset of self-heating.
