claims.quote: The lowest T0 was that of the Samsung charged Li-ion battery at 116.5 ◦C. The Samsung also had the earliest initial self-heating.

claims.claim: Before the battery temperature approaches the uncontrollable temperature, the gas release of LFP, LMO, and NCM batteries during thermal runaway is 0.094 mol, 0.042 mol, and 0.058 mol, respectively.
claims.quote: the gas release of LFP, LMO and NCM batteries are 0.094 mol, 0.042 mol and 0.058 mol, respectively

claims.claim: The gas released before safety venting accounts for 29.1%, 75.2%, and 55.4% of the total gas that causes the safety valve to open in LFP, LMO, and NCM batteries, respectively.
claims.quote: These gases account for 29.1, 75.2 and 55.4% of the gas volume that cause the safety valve to open in the LFP, LMO, NCM batteries, respectively

claims.claim: Safety venting of the LFP battery is mainly due to electrolyte volatilization, whereas safety venting of LMO and NCM batteries is mainly caused by gas release.
claims.quote: the safety venting of the LFP battery is mainly due to the volatilization of the electrolyte. However, the safety venting of the LMO and NCM batteries is mainly caused by the gas release

claims.claim: The NCM battery has the greatest total pressure change during thermal runaway at 9.817 MPa, which is 4.3 times the pressure change of the LFP battery.
claims.quote: The NCM battery has the greatest pressure change, P = 9.817 MPa, which is 4.3 times the pressure change of the LFP battery

claims.claim: For the LFP battery, gas release is the main cause of structural change after thermal runaway, whereas for LMO and NCM batteries, impact force is the dominant cause of pressure increase.
claims.quote: For the LFP battery, the gas release is found to be the main cause of the structural change, and for the LMO and NCM batteries, the impact force is the dominant cause

claims.claim: At 50% SoC, the LFP battery has the highest peak explosion pressure among LCO, NCA, and LFP battery types with the same SoC, followed by NCA, and LCO has the least peak pressure.
claims.quote: For a 50% SoC, the LFP battery has the maximum pressure among the three battery types with the same SoC, which is followed by the NCA, and, finally, the LCO has the least peak pressure among all of them.

claims.claim: At 50% SoC and a vent area of 0.36 m², the pressure rise from explosive ignition of LFP battery gases exceeds that of NMC battery at 100% SoC, indicating LFP batteries are more hazardous under this condition.
claims.quote: Fig. 4a also demonstrates that, at 50% SoC and a vent area of 0.36 m2, the pressure-rise due to explosive ignition of the gases ejected from the LFP battery exceeds that in the case of the NMC battery at 100% SOC, which indicates that the LFP batteries are more hazardous at this condition for the cases investigated.

claims.claim: For any equivalence ratio, increasing the enclosure vent area causes a monotonic reduction in peak explosion pressure.
claims.quote: For any ϕ, an increase in the enclosure vent area caused a monotonic reduction in the peak pressure.

claims.claim: For syngas, the laminar flame speed is highest at a rich condition rather than at stoichiometry, causing peak explosion pressure to be highest at fuel-rich conditions for LIB vented gas mixtures.
claims.quote: For syngas, the laminar flame speed is highest not at the stoichiometry but at a rich condition as depicted in Fig. 2d... the maximum pressure is observed at the fuel-rich condition, which is due to the syngas laminar flame speed behavior.

claims.claim: When CO2 is accounted for as part of the vented gas from LFP battery at 50% SoC, the equivalence ratio decreases by 32.4%, the largest reduction among the four battery cases considered, and the LFP switches from having the highest peak pressure to the lowest.
claims.quote: the largest change observed for 50% SoC LFP LIB, with 32.4% reduction in its equivalence ratio... The peak pressure switched from the LFP having the highest peak pressure to the lowest peak pressure as shown in Fig. 8.

claims.claim: When flaming combustion occurs during battery thermal runaway, the effective heat of combustion jumps to 50 kJ/Wh, compared to 6–12 kJ/Wh when no flaming combustion is taking place.
claims.quote: When no flaming combustion is taking place, the effective heat of combustion is comprised between 6 and 12 kJ/Wh, reflecting very incomplete combustion processes driven by semi-oxidative environment created in a first step with oxygen released by the cathodic transition metal oxide mix. In contrast, when flames are visible, the effective heat of combustion jumps to 50 kJ/Wh.

claims.claim: During the fume phase (no flames), the CO/CO2 ratio is close to 0.8, illustrating a dominant pyrolytic process, whereas in the presence of flames the CO/CO2 ratio is close to 0.01, indicating good combustion not limited by O2 availability.
claims.quote: The ratio CO/CO2 (vol.) is close to 0.01 in the presence of flame showing a good combustion, not limited by the O2 availability. On the contrary, in the first phase, when only fumes are visible, the CO/CO2 ratio is close to 0.8, illustrating a dominant pyrolytic process.

claims.claim: When flames appear during battery thermal runaway, CO2 production increases by a factor of 8, HF production increases by a factor of 10, and NOx production increases by a factor of 4, while CH4 decreases by a factor of 12, C2H4 decreases by a factor of 7, CO decreases by a factor of 10, POF3 decreases by a factor of 4, carbonates decrease by a factor of 7, and CH2O and H2 almost disappear.
claims.quote: gaseous species produced in larger quantities after the flame appearance. It is the case of CO2 (x8); HF (x10) and NOx (x4). 2- gaseous species produced in smaller quantities after the flame appearance as witnessed for CH4 (/12), C2H4 (/7), CO (/10), POF3 (/4), carbonates (/7), CH2O (almost disappearing) and H2 (almost disappearing).

claims.claim: At module level, the simultaneous reaction of two adjacent cells produced a peak heat release rate of 250 kW, compared to 100–130 kW peaks at cluster level where cells reacted sequentially, even though only 3 cells participated in the reaction in both cases.
claims.quote: At cluster level, three peaks are identifiable. A small one (20 kW), attributed to the reaction of the first cell with no flames, and two bigger ones (100–130 kW) related to the reaction of the two neighbor cells, in the presence of fire. At module level only two peaks are visible, a small one (25 kW) also associated with the reaction of the first cell with no flames, and a second large one (250 kW) due to the simultaneous reaction of the two adjacent cells.

claims.claim: The time at which individuals may experience compromised tenability is assessed to be two times shorter in the case of flaming combustion compared to cell degassing without flaming combustion, because critical HF production is bound to fire development.
claims.quote: the time at which individuals may be expected to experience compromised tenability is assessed to be two time shorter in case of fire as compared to cell degassing emission only.

claims.claim: In both air and nitrogen environments, the safety valve opening (SVO) temperature and TR onset temperature of LIB decreased with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: In air or nitrogen environment, the SVO temperature and TR onset temperature of LIB decreased with the increased of SOC.

claims.claim: In air atmosphere, LEL, UEL, and ELR of LIB-TR gas all increase with increasing SOC.
claims.quote: In air atmosphere, LEL, UEL and ELR increase with the increase of SOC, and the explosion risk of TR gas in nitrogen atmosphere is lower than that in air atmosphere.

claims.claim: The explosion risk of LIB-TR gas in nitrogen atmosphere is lower than that in air atmosphere.
claims.quote: the explosion risk of TR gas in nitrogen atmosphere is lower than that in air atmosphere.

claims.claim: At 100% SOC in air, the battery with 100% SOC consumed almost all the oxygen in the container during TR, indicating severe combustion occurred during LIB-TR.
claims.quote: The battery with 100 % SOC consumed almost all the oxygen in the container during TR, indicating that severe combustion occurred during LIB-TR.

claims.claim: Under the same SOC, the amount of gas generated by LIB-TR in nitrogen environment is less than that in air environment, because combustible gases cannot undergo oxidation reaction with O2 in nitrogen, inhibiting the chain reaction of free radicals.
claims.quote: With the same SOC, the amount of gases generated by the LIB-TR in the nitrogen environment is less than that of the air environment. In the nitrogen environment, the combustible gases generated by the reaction of materials in the battery can not undergo oxidation reaction with O2, and the chain reaction of free radicals is inhibited, thus resulting in the reduction of gas generation.

claims.claim: For NCM 811 batteries, as SOC increases, the lower explosion limit (LEL) of battery vent gas first increases and then decreases, while the upper explosion limit (UEL) continues to increase nearly linearly.
claims.quote: the LEL of BVG increased at the initial stage and then decreased with the increase of SOC of NCM 811 batteries, the UEL showed a trend of nearly linear progression with the increase of the SOC, and the explosion range became wider with the increase of the SOC.

claims.claim: The BVG explosion range of NCM 811 batteries becomes wider as the battery SOC increases.
claims.quote: it was also verified that the BVG explosion range increases as the battery SOC increases

claims.claim: The lower explosion limit of BVG follows an opposite trend to the multi-carbon chain gas fraction: as multi-carbon chain gas fraction decreases, LEL increases, reaching a maximum at 50% SOC.
claims.quote: the lower explosion limit presents an inverted tendency with the multi-carbon chain gas fraction. The lower explosion limit of BVG tends to increase as the multi-carbon chain gas fraction decreases and reaches a maximum at 50 % SOC.

claims.claim: At 50% SOC, NCM 811 batteries exhibit the highest LEL of 10.15% and the lowest explosion pressure of 0.2453 MPa at LEL, with the lowest maximum explosion flame temperature of 170.1°C, indicating the best thermal safety at this SOC.
claims.quote: Considering the 50 % SOC batteries have the highest LEL of 10.15 % with the lowest risk of combustion and explosion in an open space situation, it is recommended that 50 % SOC as a secure storage value for NCM 811 batteries... the 50 % SOC battery showing the lowest explosion pressure of 0.2453 MPa at LEL, when the maximum temperature of the explosion flame is also the lowest, at 170.1 ◦C

claims.claim: The maximum explosion pressure of NCM 811 BVG at the explosion limit is above 0.3 MPa in all experimental groups except the 50% SOC battery at LEL, which still exceeds 0.2 MPa; the 100% SOC battery at LEL shows the highest explosion pressure of 0.4585 MPa.
claims.quote: in all experimental groups the maximum explosion pressure at the explosion limit is basically above 0.3 Mpa, the minimum value is the 50 % SOC batteries at the LEL, but also more than 0.2 Mpa. The 100 % SOC battery at LEL showed the highest explosion pressure of 0.4585 Mpa

claims.claim: As SOC increases, the maximum temperature of battery thermal runaway increases, the thermal runaway onset temperature decreases, and the thermal runaway onset time advances, indicating that higher SOC batteries require less heat to trigger thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature of the battery thermal runaway process increases with higher SOC and as the SOC increases, the lower the thermal runaway onset temperature and the earlier the thermal runaway onset time, which also indicates that the less heat is required for the thermal runaway of the higher the SOC lithium-ion battery.

claims.claim: The solid-state electrolyte interphase is damaged at approximately 80–100°C, leading to an exothermic reaction between the electrolyte and the partially charged anode during thermal runaway Stage 2.
claims.quote: The solid-state electrolyte interphase is damaged at ≈80–100 ◦C, leading to an exothermic reaction between the electrolyte and (partially) charged anode.

claims.claim: The mechanical breakdown of the separator at approximately 120–150°C leads to an internal short circuit and a steep temperature rise due to large local currents during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The mechanical breakdown of the separator at ≈120–150 ◦C leads to an internal short circuit and a steep temperature rise due to large local currents.

claims.claim: Above approximately 180°C, the cathode begins to decompose exothermically and release oxygen during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Above ≈180 ◦C, the cathode begins to decompose exothermically and release oxygen.

claims.claim: A self-heating rate of approximately 0.2 K/min is considered the onset temperature of thermal runaway, while a self-heating rate of ≥10 K/min is often defined as the thermal runaway starting temperature.
claims.quote: A self-heating of ≈0.2 K min−1 is considered as onset temperature of the TR. ... A self-heating of ≥10 K min−1 is often defined as the TR starting temperature.

claims.claim: An increase of incident heat flux from 10 kW/m² to 75 kW/m² was found to significantly increase the measured surface temperature, PHRR, THR, mass loss, and CO or CO2 production.
claims.quote: for the radiant heating method an increase of the incident heat flux from 10 kW m−2 to 75 kW m−2 was found to also significantly increase the measured surface temperature, PHRR, THR, mass loss and even in the CO or CO2 production

claims.claim: Under overcharging conditions, the safety vent of the 86 Ah LFP battery opens at 1551 s when the surface temperature is 48.5°C, and a large amount of liquid electrolyte is emitted.
claims.quote: for the overcharging condition, the safety vent opens at 1551 s, when the surface temperature of the battery is 48.5 ◦C. The safety vent opens for only 1 s, and a large amount of liquid is emitted.

claims.claim: Under overheating conditions, the safety vent of the 86 Ah LFP battery opens at 842 s when the surface temperature is 94°C, releasing a large amount of white smoke over some time.
claims.quote: For the overheating condition, the safety vent opens at 842 s when the battery surface temperature is 94 ◦C. The safety vent opens, releasing a large amount of white smoke over some time.

claims.claim: Safety venting under overheating is primarily caused by electrolyte volatilization, whereas safety venting under overcharging is primarily caused by gas release inside the battery.
claims.quote: The predominant reason for the safety venting of the LFP battery is the electrolyte volatilization under overheating... However, for the overcharging condition, when the safety vent opens, the surface temperature is 48.5 ◦C, and the electrolyte ejects as a liquid. Overcharging promotes gas production inside the battery, which also shows that the main reason for the safety venting is caused by the gas release of the LFP battery.

claims.claim: The total gas volume released during thermal runaway of the 86 Ah LFP battery is 62.1 L under overcharging and 101.3 L under overheating, with CO2 and H2 accounting for the majority in both cases.
claims.quote: the total gas generations are 62.1 and 101.3 L under overcharging and overheating... CO2 and H2 account for the majority of the released gases

claims.claim: The XFED peak value under overheating (11.6) is higher than under overcharging (7.7), but XFED remains below 1 before thermal runaway under overheating, whereas under overcharging XFED exceeds 1 for 1211 s before thermal runaway occurs.
claims.quote: The peak value of XFED for the overheating condition is 11.6, which is much higher than the 7.7 for the overcharging condition... the XFED under overheating is less than 1 before TR... but the XFED of gas release is more than 1 under overcharging when TR occurs before 1211 s.

claims.claim: The peak mass loss rates under overheating (−4.3 and −5.7 g/s) are substantially higher than under overcharging (−0.53 and −1.84 g/s), and the average mass loss rate under overheating (2.25 g/s) is much higher than under overcharging (0.24 g/s).
claims.quote: The two peak mass loss rates are (cid:0) 0.53 and (cid:0) 1.84 g/s under overcharging, but the peak mass loss rates are (cid:0) 4.3 and (cid:0) 5.7 g/s under overheating... The average mass loss rates are 0.24 and 2.25 g/s under overcharging and overheating.

claims.claim: The peak surface temperature during thermal runaway is 423°C under overcharging and 372.1°C under overheating for the 86 Ah LFP battery, while the peak temperature of released gas is nearly equal at 342.4°C and 343.4°C respectively.
claims.quote: The peak surface temperature of the battery during TR under overcharging and overheating is 423 and 372.1 ◦C, respectively, and the peak temperature of gas release is 342.4 and 343.4 ◦C, respectively.

claims.claim: The onset of self-heating in the study NCA 21700 cell was detected at 86 ± 1 °C at 100% SoC, more than 35 hours before thermal runaway occurred.
claims.quote: We find that the study cell can self-heat for over 35 h before thermal runaway. This leaves more than a full day from the onset of detectable failure signals during which time mitigation strategies may be employed.

claims.claim: The maximum heating rate during thermal runaway increases exponentially with state of charge, consistent with reports on NMC cells.
claims.quote: We observe that the maximum heating rate increases exponentially with SoC, as shown in Fig. 4b, which is an agreement with other reports on NMC cells [47].

claims.claim: The venting temperature does not depend on state of charge and typically occurs around 180 °C during heat ramp tests, approximately 60 °C higher than during HWS testing.
claims.quote: The venting temperature does not appear to be dependent on the SoC, and typically occurs around 180 °C. We note that the vent temperature here is around 60 °C higher than that observed during HWS testing.

claims.claim: Mass loss during thermal runaway shows a reasonably linear dependence on state of charge, with higher SoC cells losing more mass.
claims.quote: Fig. 4d demonstrates that more cell mass is lost at higher SoC, with a reasonably linear correlation.

claims.claim: The energy transferred to heating the cell during thermal runaway is approximately 31 kJ, which is only about 50% of the total stored cell energy of approximately 60 kJ, with the residual energy attributed to ejecta.
claims.quote: The resulting distribution, shown in Fig. 5c, is centred around 31 kJ. Importantly, this is only about 50 % of the total energy stored by the cell, which is around 60 kJ. The residual energy not accounted for, around 30 kJ, is most likely attributable to the thermal runaway ejecta, both as thermal and kinetic energy.

claims.claim: The trigger method affects gas production rate, mass loss, and maximum temperature of a Li-ion cell as much as its state of charge.
claims.quote: It is concluded that the trigger method affects the gas production rate, mass loss, and maximum temperature of the cell as much as its state of charge.

claims.claim: Specific total gas production increases linearly with SOC, ranging from 0.35 L/Ah at 25% SOC to approximately 2.1 L/Ah at 100% SOC for a 157 Ah NMC811 prismatic cell.
claims.quote: the specific total gas production went from 0.35 L/Ah to 2.1 L/Ah in the range 25–100 % SOC

claims.claim: TR onset temperature decreases by 18°C per 25% increase in SOC for the tested NMC811 large format prismatic cell.
claims.quote: TR onset temperature had a decrease of 18°C per 25 % SOC increase

claims.claim: Maximum cell surface temperature during thermal runaway increases by approximately 90°C per 25% SOC increase.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature increased approximately 90°C per 25 % SOC increase

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, mass loss varied from 35% to 75% depending on the TR trigger method, while the average mass loss increase from 25% to 100% SOC was 35 percentage points.
claims.quote: the mass loss at 100 % SOC varied from 35 % to 75 % with different TR trigger methods, while the average increase for 25–100 % SOC was 35 percentage points
